title: Modelling time-irreversible avalanches
abstract: We investigate the problem of the time reversal symmetry of fluctuations, as witnessed by the average shape of avalanches. This quantity has been measured in a variety of systems, ranging from magnetic materials to earthquakes. Although an asymmetric shape is often observed, which is a signature of a non-equilibrium dynamics, there is no general theoretical control of this feature. In this paper, we propose a non equilibrium extension of a paradigmatic model for ``crackling-noise'', the so called ABBM model. Our model is strictly related to the Brownian Gyrator, which has been previously introduced in stochastic thermodynamics as the simplest model for thermal anisotropy, but it can also be framed in the context of rate-and-state models. It reproduces the phenomenology observed in experiments on granular friction, and allows for a systematic theoretical study of the asymmetry. We manage to correlate a measure of asymmetry, that can be easily computed in experiments, with the entropy production rates of the dynamics.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical model based on the Brownian Gyrator to study time-irreversible fluctuations observed in phenomena like friction and magnetic avalanches. By analyzing the average shape of these 'avalanches,' the authors quantify the degree of asymmetry. They establish a quantitative link between this measured asymmetry (skewness) and the rate of entropy production, providing a powerful diagnostic tool for non-equilibrium physics.
main_problem: The study investigates the time reversal symmetry of fluctuations, particularly by analyzing the average shape of avalanches in non-equilibrium systems.
main_result: The proposed Brownian Gyrator model successfully reproduces experimentally observed asymmetric average avalanche shapes, correlating this asymmetry with measures of entropy production rates.
method: The authors use analytical extensions to paradigmatic models (like ABBM) and compute average bridge/avalanche shapes using propagator techniques in stochastic thermodynamics.
model_or_system: The core model is an extension of the ABBM model, related to the Brownian Gyrator, which describes motion under anisotropic thermal fluctuations. It can be framed within rate-and-state friction models.
key_observables: Average avalanche/bridge shape $\langle v(t) angle_	au$, asymmetry (skewness), and entropy production rates ($S_{	ext{prod}}$).
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameters ($\gamma_x, \gamma_y$), bath temperatures ($T_x, T_y$), and the duration of the avalanche/bridge ($	au$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on extending known models to capture non-equilibrium features; numerical methods require careful handling of noise terms and integration steps.
figures_summary: Figures compare model predictions (e.g., average bridge shapes vs. duration $	au$) against granular friction experiments, showing characteristic time-reversal asymmetry and correlating maximal skewness with entropy production.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by generalizing existing models to include anisotropy, deriving equations of motion for the generalized process, calculating the average shape using propagators, and finally correlating the resulting asymmetry (skewness) with thermodynamic quantities like entropy production.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous statistical mechanical framework for quantifying non-equilibrium signatures (like time-asymmetry) in complex physical processes, linking microscopic dynamics to macroscopic irreversibility measures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Given a stochastic signal, an avalanche (red, panel a) and a bridge (green, panel b) are identified. In panels c) and d) is depicted the definition of average shape: all the events (bridges in this case) of same duration τ are plotted in grey versus the internal time (which is zero at the start of the event): at each value of the internal time the average over the collected events gives the value of the average shape (blue line), resulting in a symmetric profile (example in panel c) or an asymmetric shape (example in panel d). The orange lines represents the reversed curve (t →τ −t) and it is plotted to manifest the asymmetry. Data in figures are from arbitrary stochastic...

Figure 2. Average shapes for model (left) and granular experi- ment [40] (right). At left, the average bridge shapes computed from model in Eq. (5) are shown with increasing skewnesses (parameters: Tx = 0.1, Ty = 100, γx = 3, γy = 0.5, u = 1). In the legend the corresponding durations. At right, aver- age avalanche shapes from granular experiments with similar skewness (each curve is obtained averaging in a bracket of du- rations, as indicated in the legend) are quite similar with the scenario produced by the model.

Figure 3. Analysis of average shape skewness versus dura- tion. In panel a) results from the model (same parameters as Fig. 2): red line is the theory for average bridge shapes, black points for average avalanche shapes (numerical). In panel b) the average skewness for average avalanche shapes versus their durations from granular experiment. In the insets the corresponding distributions of avalanche duration is shown for comparison.

Figure 4. Maximal skewness of the shape observed as a func- tion of an estimation of the average entropy produced dur- ing the corresponding bridge. Points refers to more than 100000 different (random log-uniformly distributed) choices of parameters. The dashed line is a simple analytical expres- sion describing qualitatively the case (γx −γy)(Tx −Ty) ≤0. Crosses represent two systems with the same drift parameters γx, γy, and u, but temperatures swapped Tx ↔Ty (more de- tails in End Material).

Figure 5. Comparison of normalized average shapes for bridges (analytical) in red, and avalanche (numerical) in black. Param- eters: γx = γy = 1, u = 0.5, Tx = 0.125, Ty = 12.5. Only intermediate durations are shown: at smaller and larger durations, the shapes are still indistinguishable and more symmetric, with respectively parabolic and flattening shapes.
